explanation:
Express routing maps HTTP requests (method + URL pattern) to handler functions. Routes can have static paths (`/users`), named parameters (`/users/:id`), optional parameters (`/users/:id?`), and regex patterns. The router matches routes in the order they're defined — the first match wins.

Route parameters extract dynamic segments from the URL. They're available in `req.params`. Query strings are in `req.query`. The router supports all HTTP methods and can chain multiple handlers for the same route.
key_takeaway: Express routing matches first-match — define specific routes before catch-all ones. Use `express.Router()` to group related routes and mount them at a prefix.
try_it: Build a router for `/api/books` with GET (list, by ID), POST, PUT, and DELETE. Add a regex constraint that book IDs must be numeric.

explanation:
Middleware functions receive `(req, res, next)`. They can modify the request/response, end the response, or call `next()` to pass control to the next middleware. The chain executes in registration order — like an assembly line where each station inspects or modifies the product.

This pattern is the backbone of Express. Authentication, logging, validation, CORS, and error handling are all middleware. You can apply middleware globally (`app.use`), to specific routes, or to groups of routes using routers.
key_takeaway: Middleware chains execute in order — each function calls `next()` to pass control forward. Use route-specific middleware for auth and validation, global middleware for logging and CORS.
try_it: Create a middleware that times each request and adds an `X-Response-Time` header. Apply it globally and verify it works on all routes.

explanation:
The `req` object contains everything about the incoming request: URL, method, headers, query parameters, route parameters, and body (parsed by middleware). The `res` object sends the response back to the client. Express extends both with helpful methods like `res.json()`, `res.status()`, and `res.redirect()`.

Understanding `req` and `res` is essential because they're the primary interface between your application and the outside world. Every route handler receives both and must send a response — otherwise the client hangs indefinitely.
key_takeaway: `req` has everything about the incoming request (params, query, body, headers). `res` sends the response — use `.json()` for JSON, `.status()` for status codes, `.set()` for headers.
try_it: Create a `/mirror` endpoint that echoes back the request method, URL, headers, query params, and body as JSON.

explanation:
Express error-handling middleware has a special signature: four arguments `(err, req, res, next)`. When any middleware or route handler calls `next(err)` or throws an exception, Express skips all remaining normal middleware and jumps to the error handler. This centralized pattern catches all errors in one place.

Without proper error handling, unhandled errors crash the server or leave clients hanging. The pattern: create custom error classes, wrap async handlers, define a global error handler last, and add process-level handlers for uncaught exceptions and unhandled rejections.
key_takeaway: Error-handling middleware (4 args) catches all errors — use `wrap()` for async routes, custom error classes for domain errors, and never expose internal errors to clients.
try_it: Add a `wrap()` helper, create a route that throws a custom error, and verify the error handler returns a proper JSON response with the correct status code.

explanation:
Template engines generate HTML on the server by combining templates (files with placeholders) with data. Express supports many engines: EJS (embedded JavaScript), Pug (indentation-based), and Handlebars (Mustache-style). Server-side rendering (SSR) sends fully-formed HTML to the browser — good for SEO and initial page load.

EJS is the most popular because it uses plain JavaScript syntax. You set the engine with `app.set('view engine', 'ejs')`, place templates in a `views/` directory, and render them with `res.render('template', data)`. Layouts, partials, and includes keep templates DRY.
key_takeaway: Template engines render HTML on the server — EJS uses `<%= %>` for output and `<% %>` for logic. Use layouts and partials to keep templates DRY.
try_it: Create an EJS template that renders a product catalog from a JSON array, with a layout, header partial, and conditional rendering for out-of-stock items.

explanation:
Web applications face constant attacks: injection, XSS, CSRF, DDoS, and more. Express doesn't include security features by default — you must add them via middleware. The principle of defense in depth: multiple layers of security so that if one fails, others protect you.

Essential security middleware: `helmet` sets secure HTTP headers, `cors` controls cross-origin access, `express-rate-limit` prevents brute-force attacks, and `hpp` prevents HTTP parameter pollution. Input validation, parameterized queries, and HTTPS are non-negotiable.
key_takeaway: Use helmet for security headers, cors for cross-origin control, rate-limit for brute-force protection, and always validate/sanitize input. Defense in depth — no single layer is enough.
try_it: Add helmet, cors, and rate-limiting to an existing Express API. Use curl to verify the security headers are present and rate limiting works.

practice_challenges.title: CRUD API with Express Router
practice_challenges.hint: Use express.Router() to create modular route files. Map GET/POST/PUT/DELETE to controller functions. Use proper status codes: 201 for create, 204 for delete.
practice_challenges.description: Build a RESTful API for managing a todo list with Express Router. Implement routes in a separate router file: GET /todos (list with pagination), GET /todos/:id, POST /todos, PATCH /todos/:id, DELETE /todos/:id. Use an in-memory store. Add input validation with express-validator and proper error responses.

practice_challenges.title: JWT Authentication Middleware
practice_challenges.hint: Use jsonwebtoken for signing/verifying. Store refresh tokens in database. Implement access token (15min) + refresh token (7d) pattern.
practice_challenges.description: Implement JWT authentication: POST /auth/register (hash password with bcrypt), POST /auth/login (returns access + refresh tokens), POST /auth/refresh (get new access token), POST /auth/logout (invalidate refresh token). Protect routes with an auth middleware that verifies the Bearer token.

practice_challenges.title: File Upload with Streaming & Progress
practice_challenges.hint: Use multer for multipart handling. For large files, use busboy directly for streaming. Generate unique filenames with crypto.randomUUID(). Validate file type by magic bytes, not extension.
practice_challenges.description: Build a file upload service that handles multipart uploads up to 500MB. Support single and multiple file uploads. Validate file types using magic bytes (not just extensions). Generate thumbnails for images using sharp. Store files in a configurable directory with date-based organization. Return a download URL after upload.
practice_challenges.example_output: 3 files uploaded, thumbnails generated for images, response: [{"id": "...", "url": "/files/2026/05/26/abc.jpg", "thumbnail": "/files/2026/05/26/abc_thumb.jpg"}]

practice_challenges.title: Real-Time Chat with Socket.io Integration
practice_challenges.hint: Attach socket.io to the Express HTTP server. Use rooms for channels. Store message history in memory or database. Emit events for typing indicators and read receipts.
practice_challenges.description: Build a real-time chat backend: Express serves the REST API (user auth, message history, channel management) and Socket.io handles live messaging. Support: public channels, direct messages, typing indicators, read receipts, and message history pagination. Store messages in a database. Reconnection handling: when a user reconnects, deliver any missed messages.
practice_challenges.example_input: User "alice" joins #general and types "hello" — user "bob" is already in the channel
practice_challenges.example_output: Bob receives typing indicator, then the message in real-time. Alice gets delivery confirmation. Both can scroll up to see last 50 messages from history.

practice_challenges.title: API Gateway with Request Transformation
practice_challenges.hint: Use http-proxy-micro or node-http-proxy for proxying. Implement request/response transformation middleware. Use circuit breaker pattern for downstream service failures.
practice_challenges.description: Build an API gateway that: authenticates requests (JWT), applies rate limiting per client, transforms incoming requests to match downstream service contracts, aggregates responses from multiple services into a single response (BFF pattern), implements circuit breaker for failing services, and logs all requests with correlation IDs for distributed tracing.
practice_challenges.example_input: GET /api/dashboard (needs data from user-service, analytics-service, notification-service)
practice_challenges.example_output: Gateway authenticates, calls 3 services in parallel with circuit breaker, aggregates into {user: {...}, analytics: {...}, notifications: [...]}, returns with X-Correlation-Id header

explanation: Express interviews test your ability to build robust middleware pipelines, handle errors gracefully, and design clean RESTful APIs. The most common challenge is ordering middleware correctly — authentication before routes, error handlers last. Interviewers also love asking about JWT validation, file uploads, and rate limiting in real-world scenarios.
key_takeaway: Middleware order matters: parsing before validation, auth before routes, error handlers last. Always wrap async handlers to catch promise rejections.
try_it: Build a small REST API with JWT auth and error handling before checking solutions

practice_challenges.title: Logging Middleware with Timing
practice_challenges.hint: Record Date.now() at start, calculate duration when response finishes using res.on('finish', ...)
practice_challenges.description: Create a global middleware that logs each request as `[METHOD] /path — STATUS — Xms`. It should capture the start time, wait for the response to finish, then log the method, URL, status code, and elapsed time in milliseconds.

practice_challenges.title: JWT Authentication Guard
practice_challenges.hint: Extract token from Authorization: Bearer <token> header, verify with jsonwebtoken, attach decoded payload to req.user
practice_challenges.description: Write an auth middleware that extracts a JWT from the Authorization header, verifies it using a secret key, and attaches the decoded user payload to req.user. If the token is missing or invalid, respond with 401. Apply it to protect a /profile route.
practice_challenges.example_input: Authorization: Bearer eyJhbG... with valid payload {id: 1, role: 'user'}

practice_challenges.title: RESTful CRUD Router
practice_challenges.hint: Use express.Router() to group routes, validate req.params.id exists, return proper status codes (201 for POST, 204 for DELETE)
practice_challenges.description: Build a complete RESTful router for /api/products with: GET / (list with pagination via ?page=&limit=), GET /:id (single item), POST / (create with validation), PUT /:id (update), DELETE /:id (delete). Return appropriate status codes and handle not-found cases for GET/PUT/DELETE.

practice_challenges.title: File Upload with Validation
practice_challenges.hint: Use multer with fileFilter to accept only images (mimetype starts with 'image/'), set limits for file size, handle MulterError in error handler
practice_challenges.description: Create a POST /upload endpoint using multer that accepts a single image file (max 5MB). Validate the MIME type is an image (jpeg, png, gif). Return the filename and size on success. If the file is too large or wrong type, return a structured error response with the appropriate status code.
practice_challenges.example_input: Multipart form-data with file: photo.jpg (2MB, image/jpeg)

practice_challenges.title: API Rate Limiter per User
practice_challenges.hint: Use a Map to track request counts per user key, implement sliding window with timestamps array, return 429 with Retry-After header when limit exceeded
practice_challenges.description: Implement a custom rate-limiting middleware that limits each authenticated user to 10 requests per 60-second window. Use an in-memory Map keyed by user ID. Track timestamps of requests. When the limit is exceeded, respond with 429 and a Retry-After header showing seconds until the oldest request expires. Include cleanup logic to prevent memory leaks from stale entries.
practice_challenges.example_output: First 10: pass through with next(). 11th: 429 {error: 'Rate limit exceeded', retryAfter: 23}
